It's crazy to me that after watching this trailer it is essentially a compilation of the entire film quite literally in the way they arranged the moments they show in this trailer and in relation to how the movie plays out of course there are some pieces and key moments that this trailer doesn't show but I know this is likely not the only trailer and there were other ones with additional 

Never been a big fan of the alien series in general maybe it's because I'm more into slashers and horror in general and not so much the science fiction aspect of things but I really have to say although this film was definitely better than something like alien covenant I think where this film suffered is that the cast was too small had there been a few more characters a few more interactions between the xenomorphs and the characters and less emphasis on the facehuggers then I think this would have made for a much more interesting film 

What I genuinely think would be an interesting way to continue the series moving forward is definitely to find a proper way to have both xenomorphs and predators interact with each other have one last movie the focus is on perhaps a planet that is overrun by xenomorphs orders of simply xenomorphs and to collect specimens 

Of course it would be a hunting ground planet for predators and I think the best way for the film to play out is that the predators are the main villain where xenomorphs are just kind of cannon fodder

Kill count: spoilers ahead. You’ve been warned.





How many kills did this pre-sequel rack up? Let’s find out and get to the numbers.


I counted 5 deaths within Alien Romulus. 2 boys,2 girls,and 1 agender Xeno-Human hybrid.
Making a mixed red,blue,and gray pie chart.
With a run time of 119 minutes that gives us a kill on average of 23.8 minutes.

I’ll give the golden chainsaw for coolest kill to Navarro who died of the classic chest burster kill.

Dull machete for lamest kill will go to Kay who died off screen due to blood loss.

Anyway I’m Jason Hasenfus and this has been the kill count.

I watched this with my wife and friends last night in IMAX, and we all loved it. I was expecting it to be as bad as Covenant, so I was happily disappointed in that sense. It could've been longer, but 119 minutes was time enough to tell a story like this.

While many will feel like it's "fan service (it was in the positive sense)" or show the full movie in the trailer (it really didn't). In an ocean of bad horror remakes and sequels, it felt like a refreshing wave. I genuinely felt like a scared kid all over again when I watched Aliens at the theater in 1987. By the movie's end, I was excited.

There were elements being given to the Xenomorph species (I won't go into details, so you'll have to see for yourself), and tying in elements from Prometheus and Covenant, and other Easter eggs throughout. 

Fede Alvarez definitely did the series and science fiction horror as a whole some justice with this one.

I’m happy to say that #AlienRomulus  does everything a good sequel should do:

- Give the audience something new. (Get ready for neat moments that breathe new life into the series.)

- Identify what worked before and build upon it. (From the opening frame to the last, there’s a healthy balance of drama, sci-fi, horror, and action.)

- Change the stakes. (Alvarez and his team crank up the tension, keeping you on the edge of your seat.)

- Play with expectations. (Prepare for twists and turns that will keep you guessing.)

- Add new, memorable characters. (They add depth to the story and leave a lasting impression on you.)

I'm not a huge fan of the super young crew ("kids") or the 'WOAH' guy either, but the acting looks good, and I could not have asked for better visuals, sound effects, and atmosphere. This could be the kickstart of a franchise that many of us thought would never be revived, much due to the perfection of the first two films, which set standards that are impossible to meet. If we can get anywhere close, then I'll be very happy and look forward to the next.

I have seen the making of Aliens more times than you can tell. Here’s the secret, and you can verify it by any “making of Aliens” video on YouTube: The whole scene was shot backwards. In the movie Ripley is shown crawling away fro the facehugger while the face hugger is advancing. How they made that scene was that Sigourney was actually crawling towards the face hugger and the face hugger was being pulled away. If you reverse or see it backwards, it’s gonna show as Ripley crawling away from the face hugger with the face hugger crawling towards her. 

Many scenes in that sequence were shot in reverse. Like when the face hugger wraps it’s tail around Sigourney’s neck. In actuality, they first wrapped the tail around her neck and then filmed it as unwrapping around the neck, then reversing it to show that it’s wrapping around the neck. 

Also the face hugger was thrown at her, but by good editing it seems like a whole uncut sequence. Basically the only CGI, or “digital” as they called it back then, was when the drop ship drop ships from the Nostromo and enters the planet. They used blue screen instead of green screen back then. It looks horrible and I was hoping Cameron would change it for the 4k release.

I don’t know why I bothered explaining filming to you. You either have an eye for it or you do not. In your case you do not.

I think people misunderstood what made Alien scary. "Fear of the unknown" is not necessarily what makes cosmic horror scary. What made covenant less of a cosmic horror is how the story and visuals are presented. the aliens are just humanoids with greek culture instead of actually being alien, like in the original. The xenomorphs are presented as having simple enough biological functions for humans to create, as opposed to beings that are born from technology/culture that are far removed from our own understanding/capacity. Creatures of prometheus/covenant feel much less scary because they reflect humanity too much.

If we replace H.P. lovecraft's creatures with greek gods, or some other pop culture tropes like your standard grey-aliens,  I'm sure his stories will not count as cosmic horror anymore.
Lets hope Alvares understands this.

The sound effects of the facehugger and chestburster are brutal, love it. I'm so happy to see the franchise in the hands of someone who truly gets why Alien and Aliens are such masterpieces in their own right. You can literally watch Aliens first and Alien second and it would take away little from the overall experience. To (seemingly) be able to capture  the essence of both movies and still manage to bring your own style into it by for instance leaning into the body horror. Then even go big on the practical and not get caught into doing it all with CGI. Fede is already making history within the franchise with what we've seen so far, that is impressive. I thought I'd never see the day that I would be counting the days to go see an Alien movie once again. I thought that ship had sailed and the abstract vision Ridley Scott gave us deserves praise too. Despite how predictable the plot was in Prometheus and Covenant the path was all but predictable and that made them very interesting to theorize over. But no matter if an alien bursted out of someone's back, throat, traditionally their chest or was surgically extracted. It always felt rather tame and obligatory. Somehow Fede managed to show us in these trailers multiple obligatory alien body horror moments and proves without a shadow of a doubt that as a director you don't have to drastically change a good idea in order to reinvent it.

0:06 Huh??? Did they establish a colony on the planet Kalpamos instead of the satellite LV-426? It looks very similar to Kalpamos. The planet's rings are also...
It might not be Kalpamos...but if it were Kalpamos out of nowhere, it would be a very exciting story~ For reference, the planet Kalpamos has 3 satellites.
The satellite on the far left is LV-426, which is the background of Alien 1 and 2, and the satellite on the far right, which orbits the outermost part, is LV-223, which appeared in the prequel Prometheus. I was hoping that the satellite in Prometheus was LV-426... It turned out to be LV-223 unexpectedly, so my curiosity about the LV-426 prequel in Alien 1 is still unanswered..
I thought it would be revealed in the prequel 3, but it was overturned, which is a shame.. This Romulus can be considered as 1.5 in terms of timeline.

I find it so strange the way all Hollywood and streaming series writers and production designers now seem like the same ones... Imagine the original masterpiece from 1979 having fresh-faced kids carefully covered in obvious make-up trampling on it's stunningly crafted suspense scenes with cheaply read lines like, "WTF was that?". It's as if they can't stand the idea of a character just being quiet and the actor actually acting the suspense with the visual media of film. It always has to be teenagers going, "Oh em gee!" or something. 

And why does every character in literally everything look like a Gap model or something? The original movie looked lived in, dirty, grimy, real, and the characters felt like real complex and interesting people, each really unique, not homogenous and bland Instantgram posers that always just look like actors acting. Well, at least this looks like everything else from Star Wars and Star Trek to any other production that all use the exact same templated camerawork, editing, lighting, set and costume design and so on and so on...

Also, the whole Alien thing has been done. There is nothing fresh or of any real value to add to these stories, they've been done and frankly done todeath at this point too. Create your own new and original films. The whole reason masterpieces like Alien were so great and popular in the first place was because they were highly original at the time, fresh and bold new ideas. Not just repeatedly copying the same old things ad nauseam to spin out franchise profits without any need for hiring and paying real artists and craftspeople to do anything fresh, daring and surprising with their fiction works.
